english: Now, there was in olden times etc. Dzl. and elsewh., frq.; loṅ źig daṅ ṅá-la dbaṅ yod (long zhig dang nga la dbang yod) do take it! I have the power, you know, i.e. I shall answer for it Dzl.; in more recent times it is used (also when not followed by any other words) as an imperative particle = ċig (cig): 'da zo daṅ' byas-pas ('da zo dang' byas pas) saying 'eat!' Glr.; 'da ltos daṅ' gsuṅs ('da ltos dang' gsungs) 'now just see', he said Mil.; even after źu (zhu), which in its application is like a verb in the imperative: 'gsuṅ-ba źu daṅ' źes zér-bas ('gsung ba zhu dang' zhes zer bas) saying 'pray, teach (us)!' Mil. — 6. In W. daṅ (dang) is used improperly for the instrum.: *bér-ka daṅ duṅ* (*ber ka dang dung*) strike with the stick! and for by or through with respect to persons: *yóg-po daṅ śab-śób zer* (*yog po dang shab shob zer*) he cheats me, tells me a lie, through his servant.

1. meadow Lh. — 2. daṅ (dang), or perh. better taṅ (tang), (cf. tiṅ (ting)), *taṅ taṅ-ċe* (*tang tang ce*), or taṅ ċo-ċe (tang co ce), taṅ śaṅ ċo-ċe* (tang shang co ce*), to read in a singing or drawling manner Ld. — 3. daṅ-du lén-pa (dang du len pa), c. la, to submit, yield to, comply with, Glr., Tar.; c. accus submissively to put up with (Sch. and Wts. are hardly right).
1. appetite, daṅ-ga 'gag (dang ga 'gag) my appetite is gone, mi bde (mi bde) is bad, Med. and Mil. (Sch. 'the will'?). — 2. C. for dám-k'a (dam kha).

1. the first, with respect to number, time, rank, dáṅ-poï gtam (dang po'i gtam) de sus zer (de sus zer) Pth. who spoke (raised) the first rumour? who was it that first got up the rumour? dáṅ-poï nyin-par (dang po'i nyin par) on the very first day; na-tsód dáṅ-po-la gnás-pa (na tshod dang po la gnas pa) being still in the prime of life Wdn.; the former, he that is mentioned before another, dáṅ-po gnyis (dang po gnyis) the two first named Thgy.; the former, the earlier, he that precedes another in point of time, = sṅá-ma (snga ma), opp. to pýi-ma (phyi ma), 'óg-ma ('og ma), the latter. — 2. the first thing, part etc., nyín-moï dáṅ-po-la (nyin mo'i dang po la) at the beginning of day, at day-break Tar.; daṅ-po-nyíd-du (dang po nyid du) in the first place, before the rest, above all, before every other thing Thgy.; dáṅ-po-nas (dang po nas) from the very beginning Thgy., Tar.; dáṅ-por (dang por), and very frq. dáṅ-po (dang po) adv., firstly, in the first place; at first, in the beginning. — las-dáṅ-po-pa (las dang po pa) a beginner, las-dáṅ-po-paï dús-su (las dang po pa'i dus su) as long as he is only a beginner Thgy.; las-dáṅ-po-paï byís-pa (las dang po pa'i byis pa) like νήπιος (child) in the N.T., Mil.

1. to be pure, nám-mk'a dáṅ-nas (nam mkha' dang nas) Mil.; gen. adj. pure, clear, 'bras dáṅ-ba ('bras dang ba) picked rice Lt.; of inclinations, dispositions, feelings: séms-can kún-la rab dáṅ-ba (sems can kun la rab dang ba) full of love towards all creatures; dge-séms dáṅ-ba (dge sems dang ba) a pure, sincere disposition to virtue S.O.; most frq. devout, pious; devotion, faith; dáṅ-baï sems (dang ba'i sems) id. (in W. often confounded with gdeṅ-ba (gdeng ba)). — 2. lag dáṅ-ba (lag dang ba) = dár-ba (dar ba), v. darba (dar ba) II. 2.
(spelling dubious) stable, for cattle, C., W.
1. Sch. 'a tract of land abounding in springs'. — 2. n. of a high mountain pass, north of Lhasa, called Tantla by Huc II., 231.

C. also *dhaṅ-po* (*dhang po*), pure, clean, clear, = daṅ-ba (dang ba) I., of air, water; rnam-dwáṅs (rnam dwangs) a clear sky, fine weather (W. *taṅ* (*tang*)); daṅs-smug (dangs smug) reddish gray Sch. — dwaṅs-ma (dwangs ma) 1. the chyle, Ssk. रस, concerning which Brahmanical and Buddhist physiology has led to a great many fantastical ideas, Med. frq.; also fig., mostly in an obscure and unintelligible manner. — 2. Sch.: 'the spirit, the soul', a signification not found hitherto in any book, but acc. to a Lama's statement the word denotes the soul, when purified from every sin, and to be compared to a clear and limpid fluid, in which every heterogeneous matter has been precipitated. — daṅs (dangs) is also not seldom met with erron. used for dṅaṅs (dngangs) and mdaṅs (mdangs).

1. secondary form of 'dód-pa ('dod pa) to wish Dzl. and elsewh.; hence in compounds: skom-dád (skom dad) thirst, *tágs-dad-can* (*tags dad can*) fond of dress or finery (cf. 'dogs-pa ('dogs pa)) W., and in similar expressions. — 2. to believe (cf. श्रद्धा) in a religious sense, more significant than yid-čes-pas (yid ches pas) and including a devotedness full of confidence, like πιστεύειν in the N.T.; also sbst. faith, more fully dád-
